message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 39
Recent Green phase: No code change — "damage to a rune (value 250), damage 200 → payout 100" passed immediately, confirming components (runes) with no enchantment/material receive standard full reimbursement minus deductible.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. No code changed this cycle; document if nothing is warranted.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 39 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the code for refactoring opportunities.

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**

The recent Green phase confirmed that runes (components) with no enchantment/material get standard full reimbursement minus deductible — no code changed. The relevant code path is `damagePayout` → `reimbursableAmount` → `hasReducedReimbursement`.

Let me evaluate the names touched by this cycle:

- `hasReducedReimbursement(item)` — returns true when enchantment ≥ 8. The name reveals intent well: an item with high enchantment gets reduced reimbursement. For a rune (no enchantment), `enchantmentLevel` returns 0, so this is false → full reimbursement. Name is accurate.
- `reimbursableAmount(damage, item)` — returns the reimbursable portion before deductible. Clear.
- `damagePayout(damage, items)` — finds the matching item, applies deductible floor at 0. Clear.

The names accurately describe behavior including the new rune case. No rename warranted.

**Step 2: Looking for a concrete improvement**

Let me look at one duplication candidate I noticed. The `lookupByType` helper is used for both premium base (`unitBase`) and insurance value (`insuranceValue`), which is good DRY. 

Let me look at `validateDamageCounts` more closely — it uses an imperative `for...of` loop with an early `throw`. Compare with `validateDamageAmounts` and `validateKnownTypes` which both use the `find`-then-throw pattern. There's a stylistic inconsistency, but the `for...of` here iterates a Map and needs both key and value — `find` is awkward for that. Let me check whether converting it improves both consistency (Rule 2) and APP mass.

**Step 3: APP Mass — `validateDamageCounts` (current)**

```
const validateDamageCounts = (damages, items) => {
  const insuredCounts = countByType(items);                    // binding(1) + invocation(2) = 3
  const damageCounts = countBy(damages, (d) => d.itemType);    // binding(1) + invocation(2) + binding param(1) + binding(1) = 5
  for (const [itemType, damaged] of damageCounts) {            // loop(5) + 2 bindings(2) = 7
    if (damaged > (insuredCounts.get(itemType) ?? 0)) {        // conditional(4) + invocation(2) + constant(1) = 7
      throw new Error(`... "${itemType}" ...`);                // invocation(2) + binding(1) = 3
    }
  }
};
```
Approx total: 25.

The `find`-pattern version would still need to look up insured counts per entry. Converting Map iteration to `find` over `[...damageCounts]` would add a spread invocation and a destructure inside the predicate — not clearly lower mass, and arguably less clear since `find` discards the match reason. This change does not improve clarity or mass; I'll leave it.

Let me look at the most promising target: the `lookupByType` indirection. There are three lookup helpers (`lookupByType`, `unitBase`, `baseForType`) that interact. Let me examine `baseForType`:

```
const baseForType = (type, count) =>
  COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) ? componentBase(count) : unitBase(type) * count;
```

This re-checks `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type)`, and `unitBase` internally calls `lookupByType` which checks `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type)` *again*. So for a non-component, the membership test runs twice. That's conceptual duplication (Rule 3) and redundant work.

Let me trace it precisely. For a component type, `baseForType` returns `componentBase(count)` and `unitBase` is never called. For a non-component, `baseForType` calls `unitBase(type)` which calls `lookupByType(type, COMPONENT_BASE, BASE_PREMIUMS)` — and since `type` is not a component, the ternary in `lookupByType` evaluates `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type)` (false) and returns `BASE_PREMIUMS[type] ?? 0`. So the `COMPONENT_TYPES.has` check is genuinely performed twice for non-components.

A cleaner formulation: since `baseForType` already branches on `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type)`, the non-component branch can look up `BASE_PREMIUMS` directly rather than routing through `unitBase`/`lookupByType`. But `unitBase` is also used independently in `itemSurcharges` (per-item surcharge base), where the `lookupByType` indirection IS needed (it must resolve component vs main for a single item type). So `unitBase`/`lookupByType` must stay for that caller.

This is a genuine but minor double-evaluation. Refactoring `baseForType` to avoid it would mean duplicating the `BASE_PREMIUMS[type] ?? 0` lookup logic that `unitBase` encapsulates — trading one form of duplication for another, and reducing clarity. The current form reads cleanly as "components use block pricing; everything else uses per-unit base × count." Rule 2 (clarity) favors keeping it.

**Step 4: A real, safe improvement**

Let me reconsider `surchargeRate`:

```
const surchargeRate = (item: Item): number =>
  (item.cursed ? CURSE_RATE : 0) +
  (isHighlyEnchanted(item) ? HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE : 0);
```

This is clear and minimal. Two conditionals, but each maps to a distinct business rule. No improvement.

Let me check the `enchantmentLevel` helper signature — it accepts `Item | undefined`, used by both `isHighlyEnchanted(item: Item)` and `hasReducedReimbursement(item: Item | undefined)`. The `undefined` tolerance exists because `damagePayout` does `items.find(...)` which can return `undefined`. This is correct and necessary. No change.

I've evaluated naming, duplication, simplification, and APP mass across the modules touched this cycle and the surrounding helpers. The one real finding (double `COMPONENT_TYPES.has` evaluation in `baseForType`) cannot be removed without either duplicating the `BASE_PREMIUMS` lookup or harming the clarity that makes the component/main split readable — and `unitBase`/`lookupByType` must remain regardless for the `itemSurcharges` caller. Rule 2 (reveals intent) outranks the marginal APP/efficiency gain.

Per the mandatory-attempt rule, let me confirm there's no zero-risk clarity win available. Let me re-read the two constants near the reimbursement logic that this cycle exercised.

The reimbursement constants are named and grouped well (`DEDUCTIBLE`, `REIMBURSE_RATE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSE_THRESHOLD`, `COMPONENT_INSURANCE_VALUE`). The rune case flows through these with no special-casing — exactly the design intent.

Let me run the tests one final time to confirm green is preserved (no changes were made, so this is the sanity confirmation).

content.text:
Refactoring evaluation complete.

## Refactoring Report

**This cycle's Green phase changed no code** — the rune test ("damage to a rune, value 250, damage 200 → payout 100") passed immediately, confirming that components with no enchantment/material flow through the standard reimbursement path (`damagePayout` → `reimbursableAmount` → `hasReducedReimbursement`) with no special-casing.

### Step 1 — Naming Evaluation (first priority)
The helpers on the exercised path were checked against the now-confirmed behavior:
- `hasReducedReimbursement(item)` — true only when enchantment ≥ 8; for a rune `enchantmentLevel` is 0, so false → full reimbursement. Name accurately reveals intent.
- `reimbursableAmount`, `damagePayout` — accurate.

No rename warranted.

### Step 2/3 — Duplication & Simplification
Candidates evaluated and rejected:
- **`baseForType` double-evaluates `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type)`** (once in its own ternary, again inside `unitBase` → `lookupByType`). Removing it requires either duplicating the `BASE_PREMIUMS[type] ?? 0` lookup that `unitBase` encapsulates, or inlining — both harm clarity, and `unitBase`/`lookupByType` must stay regardless for their `itemSurcharges` caller. Rule 2 (reveals intent) outranks the marginal APP/efficiency gain.
- **`validateDamageCounts` uses `for...of` while sibling validators use `find`-then-throw.** The Map iteration needs both key and value; converting to `find` over a spread adds an invocation and a destructure-in-predicate while discarding the match reason — no clarity or mass improvement.

### APP Mass
No change made, so no before/after delta. `validateDamageCounts` (the highest-mass non-orchestration helper) measures ~25; it is already near-minimal for iterating a Map with per-key comparison.

### Outcome
- **Refactoring**: none applied — every candidate either reduced clarity (Rule 2 violation) or offered no measurable mass/clarity gain.
- **Tests**: 39 passing, 1 todo. All green ✅

Files reviewed:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_05-27-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_05-27-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

The implementation is in a clean state; deeper structural changes are better deferred to the end-refactor pass with full metric tooling.
